aliases: Al Hamas Harakat Al Muqawamah Al Islamiyyah
nodes.context: Jihadist militant and pirate group based in the Philippines, involved in bombings, kidnappings, and extortion.
Global jihadist movement; Abu Sayyaf pledged allegiance to its leader in 2014.
Main Muslim rebel group in the Philippines in the 1970s; some members later joined Abu Sayyaf.
Moro rebel group; denies links with Abu Sayyaf but military claims elements provided support.
Indonesian terrorist group; trained Abu Sayyaf members and considered linked by Philippine government.
Global jihadist network; provided initial funding and training to Abu Sayyaf.
Islamic extremist group based in Lanao del Sur; fought alongside Abu Sayyaf in the Battle of Marawi.
Criminal organization listed as an ally of Abu Sayyaf in the infobox.

aliases: Al Hamas Harakat Al Muqawamah Al Islamiyyah
nodes.context: Jihadist militant and pirate group based in the Philippines, involved in bombings, kidnappings, and drug trafficking.
Moro rebel group in the Philippines; some renegade members suspected of involvement in a kidnapping with Abu Sayyaf.
Kidnap-for-ransom group in the Philippines; member Samad Awang was involved in kidnappings with Abu Sayyaf.

aliases: Al Hamas Harakat Al Muqawamah Al Islamiyyah
nodes.context: Jihadist militant and pirate group based in the Philippines, involved in bombings, kidnappings, and drug trafficking.
Chinese criminal organization that coordinated with Abu Sayyaf in kidnappings and provided military support.
Former rebel group in the Philippines; a former leader was killed and linked to Abu Sayyaf.
Criminal gang involved in kidnappings and drug operations in the Philippines.
Criminal syndicate led by Abner Gumandol, involved in kidnapping.

aliases: Al Hamas Harakat Al Muqawamah Al Islamiyyah
nodes.context: Jihadist militant and pirate group based in the Philippines, involved in bombings, kidnappings, and extortion.
Group allied to Abu Sayyaf, claimed responsibility for the 2016 Davao City bombing.
Moro separatist group in the Philippines that condemned Abu Sayyaf's attacks on civilians.
Moro separatist group in the Philippines that condemned Abu Sayyaf and labeled them 'anti-Islam'.
IS-linked militant group that emerged in 2016 in the Philippines.

edges.evidence_quote: On July 23, 2014, Isnilon Hapilon, one of the group's leaders, swore an oath of loyalty to Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, the leader of the Islamic State (IS) officially aligning the group with the global jihadist movement.
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf pledged allegiance to Islamic State, becoming its East Asia Province.

edges.evidence_quote: Abdurajak Abubakar Janjalani's first recruits were soldiers of the MNLF and the Moro Islamic Liberation Front (MILF).
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf recruited members from MNLF.

edges.evidence_quote: Abdurajak Abubakar Janjalani's first recruits were soldiers of the MNLF and the Moro Islamic Liberation Front (MILF).
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf recruited members from MILF.

edges.evidence_quote: The group was reported to have trained alongside Jemaah Islamiyah militants.
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf trained with Jemaah Islamiyah.

edges.evidence_quote: The government noted that initial funding for ASG came from al-Qaeda through the brother-in-law of Osama bin Laden, Mohammed Jamal Khalifa.
edges.context: Al-Qaeda provided initial funding to Abu Sayyaf.

edges.evidence_quote: In May 2017, Hapilon and other members of Abu Sayyaf joined the Islamic extremist Maute Group based in Lanao del Sur during their attempt to seize control of Marawi City.
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf fought alongside the Maute Group in the Battle of Marawi.
14K is listed as an ally of Abu Sayyaf in the infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: On October 11, 2009, Irish Catholic missionary Michael Sinnott, aged 79, from Barntown County Wexford was kidnapped from a gated compound in Pagadian, the capital of Zamboanga del Sur province, suspected to be part of ASG and some renegade members of MILF.
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf and renegade MILF members were suspected of jointly kidnapping a priest.

edges.evidence_quote: In December 2020, Samad Awang, alias Ahmad Jamal, of the Abdussalam kidnap-for-ransom group was killed in a firefight with government troops in Zamboanga City. Awang was reportedly involved in the kidnapping of Italian missionary Fr. Giancarlo Bossi in 2007, businessman Joel Endino in 2011, and teacher Kathy Kasipong in 2013.
edges.context: A member of the Abdussalam group was involved in kidnappings alongside Abu Sayyaf.

edges.evidence_quote: Abu Sayyaf coordinated with the Chinese 14K Triad gang in carrying out the kidnappings. The 14K Triad has militarily supported Abu Sayyaf.
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf coordinated with 14K Triad for kidnappings and received military support from them.

edges.evidence_quote: Military sources allege Barahama Alih Group was responsible for a number of kidnapping incidents in previous years including the abduction of Australian national Warren Rodwell, Italian priest Giancarlo Bossi, and some local Filipinos.
edges.context: Barahama Alih Group is alleged to have been responsible for kidnappings, but no direct relationship with Abu Sayyaf is stated.

edges.evidence_quote: Abu Sayyaf spokesperson Abu Rami was reported to claim responsibility. He later denied the report and any involvement, saying a group allied to them; the Daulat Ul-Islamiya were responsible.
edges.context: Abu Sayyaf claimed that Daulat Ul-Islamiya, an allied group, was responsible for the Davao City bombing.

edges.evidence_quote: The terrorism against civilians committed were condemned by MNLF and MILF, who said that Abu Sayyaf strayed from their real paths of struggle
edges.context: MNLF condemned Abu Sayyaf's attacks on civilians, stating the group caused chaos.

edges.evidence_quote: The terrorism against civilians committed were condemned by MNLF and MILF, who said that Abu Sayyaf strayed from their real paths of struggle, with MILF labeling Abu Sayyaf as 'anti-Islam'
edges.context: MILF condemned Abu Sayyaf and labeled them 'anti-Islam'.
